title: How Britain lost the art of economic warfare
gist: As the US and EU build all-of-government economic-security architectures, Britain has no overall strategy and remains wedded to a just-in-time, laissez-faire model despite being one of the G7's most coercion-vulnerable economies, exposed via openness, island geography, undersea cables and non-alignment between the US, EU and China.

insights.claim: Washington has built an all-of-government economic-security apparatus treating trade, finance, technology and investment as levers of national power, spined by OFAC sanctions and an expanded CFIUS plus CHIPS supply-chain protection.
insights.speaker: Oliver Harvey and Sahil Mahtani
insights.so_what: Defines the US model that Britain is being measured against and lagging behind.
insights.quote: a genuinely all-of-government economic security apparatus - one that treats trade, finance, technology and investment flows as levers of national power

insights.claim: The EU's economic-security architecture includes an Anti-Coercion Instrument (nearly triggered by Trump's Greenland tariff threats), tightened investment screening, the 2023 CHIPS Act and 2024 Critical Raw Materials Act.
insights.speaker: Oliver Harvey and Sahil Mahtani
insights.so_what: Maps the concrete EU toolkit for retaliating against and de-risking from coercion.
insights.quote: nearly triggered by President Trump's tariff threats over Greenland

insights.claim: Western powers are having to become a little more like China, which has deployed economic power as a tool of planning and foreign policy for decades, in order to compete with it.
insights.speaker: Oliver Harvey and Sahil Mahtani
insights.so_what: Frames the global shift toward statecraft as convergence on Chinese-style economic intervention.
insights.quote: the Western powers are having to become a little more like China in order to compete with it
insights.locator: Indeed, one way of framing recent developments

insights.claim: Britain has no overall economic security strategy; its toolkit is limited and disjointed, with sanctions and investment screening split across the Treasury, Cabinet Office, HMRC and other departments, and threat assessment scattered over five reviews.
insights.speaker: Oliver Harvey and Sahil Mahtani
insights.so_what: The core diagnosis: UK economic security is fragmented and uncoordinated relative to peers.
insights.quote: the UK has no overall economic security strategy

insights.claim: Britain weakened rather than strengthened its central wiring by abolishing the Economic Sub-Committee of the National Security Council in a bureaucratic shuffle.
insights.speaker: Oliver Harvey and Sahil Mahtani
insights.so_what: Concrete evidence the UK is moving backward on economic-security coordination.
insights.quote: the Economic Sub-Committee of the National Security Council was abolished
insights.locator: Meanwhile, at the centre of government

insights.claim: The authors define economic security as 'the unimpeded functioning of the United Kingdom as an effective and efficient economy', borrowing the Netherlands' 2022 definition, and argue that if you cannot name what you are doing you do not know what you are doing.
insights.speaker: Oliver Harvey and Sahil Mahtani
insights.so_what: Supplies a usable working definition the UK's own reports refused to settle on.
insights.quote: if you cannot name what you are doing, you do not know what you are doing
insights.locator: We would put it another way

insights.claim: Britain's openness is a vulnerability: import dependency for basic materials rose to 28% from 3% between 2000 and 2023, and it runs persistent current account deficits averaging ~3% of GDP since 2000.
insights.speaker: Oliver Harvey and Sahil Mahtani
insights.so_what: Quantifies rising UK external dependence and financing needs that magnify coercion exposure.
insights.quote: rose to 28 per cent from three per cent even as total consumption fell

insights.claim: Britain's island geography means it can diversify suppliers but cannot diversify from being cut off by sea, and the constraint now applies to data, with the UK's internet relying almost entirely on subsea cables.
insights.speaker: Oliver Harvey and Sahil Mahtani
insights.so_what: Reframes undersea infrastructure as strategic terrain rather than 'benign plumbing'.
insights.quote: It must treat undersea infrastructure as strategic terrain rather than benign plumbing

insights.claim: Britain is dangerously non-aligned: enmeshed with the EU but outside its single market, tied to the US but without US-style insulation, and increasingly dependent on China's economy (e.g. renewables), risking becoming a client state of all three.
insights.speaker: Oliver Harvey and Sahil Mahtani
insights.so_what: Identifies UK strategic homelessness as a core coercion risk across all major blocs.
insights.quote: Britain is vulnerable to becoming a client state with regard to all three.

themes.theme: Energy, minerals and the metal-intensive transition

insights.claim: The energy transition has cemented critical minerals as central to economic strategy because it is metal-intensive (EVs use four times as much copper as petrol cars), with mining and refining concentrated in the top three countries per metal.
insights.speaker: Oliver Harvey and Sahil Mahtani
insights.so_what: Links the green transition directly to concentrated-supply chokepoint risk, a recurring investment theme.
insights.quote: EVs use four times as much copper as petrol-based cars
insights.locator: A notable instance is the energy transition

insights.claim: After Houthi Red Sea attacks, shipping insurance costs for that corridor more than doubled, exactly the kind of price shock that disproportionately hurts an import-reliant island running just-in-time supply chains.
insights.speaker: Oliver Harvey and Sahil Mahtani
insights.so_what: A live example of how chokepoint disruptions transmit into UK import costs.
insights.quote: insurance costs for shipping through that corridor more than doubled
insights.locator: After Houthi attacks in the Red Sea

insights.claim: Britain lacks significant global market power on either supply or demand side across energy, minerals and manufactured goods, and its sanctioning power is far weaker than the US's or EU's despite London's financial centre status.
insights.speaker: Oliver Harvey and Sahil Mahtani
insights.so_what: Tempers UK ambition: it must pick targeted interventions, not emulate great-power coercion.
insights.quote: Britain does not possess significant global market power

insights.claim: Britain should intervene only where it can harness relative endowments and improve resilience together (e.g. domestic payment infrastructure, North Sea minerals) and avoid white elephants like building an advanced semiconductor vertical from scratch.
insights.speaker: Oliver Harvey and Sahil Mahtani
insights.so_what: A decision rule for where state economic-security intervention is and is not worthwhile.
insights.quote: creating an advanced semiconductor vertical supply chain from scratch would clearly be an impossibly resource-intensive and inefficient white elephant
insights.locator: Britain needs to be clear about where state intervention

insights.claim: The UK has uncritically replaced reliance on Russian gas with US gas, a dependency stimulating debate in EU states facing US coercion but not in Britain, given the axiomatic status of the 'Special Relationship'.
insights.speaker: Oliver Harvey and Sahil Mahtani
insights.so_what: Flags an underexamined UK energy dependency on the US as a latent coercion vector.
insights.quote: the UK has replaced its reliance on Russian gas supplies with US ones
insights.locator: But to take just one example

insights.claim: Laissez-faire thinking that enriched Britain at the 19th-century technological frontier may now hobble it as it falls behind, in a procyclical spiral, making a shift toward economic-security thinking inevitable.
insights.speaker: Oliver Harvey and Sahil Mahtani
insights.so_what: Argues the UK doctrinal shift is coming regardless, framing timing as the open question.
insights.quote: may now threaten to hobble it as it falls further behind, in a procyclical spiral
